scenarioDescription: A company discovers that an IAM access key has been publicly exposed on GitHub. The security team must immediately disable the compromised credential, investigate all API calls made with that key, determine the blast radius of affected resources, and set up automated detection for future credential exposures.
toolboxServices: AWS IAM|AWS CloudTrail|Amazon GuardDuty|AWS Secrets Manager|Amazon Detective|AWS Config|Amazon Macie
correctPipelineOrder: AWS IAM|AWS CloudTrail|Amazon Detective|Amazon GuardDuty
hint: The first action is always to disable the compromised credential in IAM to stop the bleeding before investigating.
hint2: CloudTrail provides the API call history for the compromised key, and Detective visualizes the blast radius across related resources and accounts.
explanation: AWS IAM is the immediate first step — deactivate the exposed access key and create a new one to stop unauthorized access. AWS CloudTrail provides the complete audit trail of every API call made with the compromised key, revealing what actions the attacker performed. Amazon Detective analyzes CloudTrail data to visualize the blast radius — mapping affected resources, IP addresses, and API patterns into an interactive investigation graph. Amazon GuardDuty provides continuous monitoring with credential exfiltration detection (UnauthorizedAccess:IAMUser/InstanceCredentialExfiltration) to automatically detect future compromised credentials.

scenarioDescription: A financial institution needs to encrypt all data at rest in S3 with customer-managed keys, enforce that no bucket can ever be made public, automatically detect and classify sensitive financial data (PII, credit card numbers), and alert the security team when unencrypted objects are uploaded.
toolboxServices: AWS KMS|Amazon S3|Amazon Macie|AWS Config|S3 Block Public Access|AWS CloudTrail|Amazon Inspector
correctPipelineOrder: S3 Block Public Access|AWS KMS|Amazon Macie|AWS Config
hint: Block Public Access is a preventive control applied at the account or bucket level before any data is stored.
hint2: Macie uses ML to automatically discover and classify sensitive data patterns like PII and credit card numbers in S3 buckets.
explanation: S3 Block Public Access is enabled at the account level as the first preventive guardrail, ensuring no bucket can ever be made public regardless of individual bucket policies. AWS KMS customer-managed keys (CMKs) provide encryption at rest with full key lifecycle control, rotation policies, and audit trails — S3 bucket policies enforce aws:kms encryption on all PutObject requests. Amazon Macie continuously scans S3 buckets using ML to automatically discover and classify sensitive financial data (PII, credit card numbers, bank account numbers) and generates findings for policy violations. AWS Config rules (s3-bucket-server-side-encryption-enabled, s3-bucket-public-read-prohibited) continuously evaluate bucket compliance and trigger SNS alerts when unencrypted objects are detected.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to implement a centralized security monitoring architecture that aggregates findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, IAM Access Analyzer, and Firewall Manager across 200 accounts. Findings must be normalized, prioritized by severity, and trigger automated remediation for critical issues.
toolboxServices: AWS Security Hub|Amazon GuardDuty|Amazon EventBridge|AWS Lambda|AWS Organizations|AWS Systems Manager|Amazon SNS
correctPipelineOrder: AWS Security Hub|Amazon EventBridge|AWS Lambda|Amazon SNS
hint: Security Hub aggregates and normalizes findings from multiple security services into the AWS Security Finding Format (ASFF).
hint2: EventBridge rules match Security Hub findings by severity and finding type, routing critical findings to Lambda for automated remediation.
explanation: AWS Security Hub aggregates findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, IAM Access Analyzer, and Firewall Manager across all 200 accounts using delegated administrator, normalizing them into the AWS Security Finding Format (ASFF) with severity scoring and compliance benchmarks (CIS, PCI DSS). Amazon EventBridge captures Security Hub findings via custom rules that match on severity (CRITICAL/HIGH) and finding type, enabling event-driven automated response workflows. AWS Lambda executes remediation actions — isolating compromised instances, revoking IAM credentials, blocking malicious IPs in WAF, or quarantining S3 buckets — based on the finding type. Amazon SNS sends formatted notifications to the security team's Slack/PagerDuty channels for findings requiring human review.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to implement cross-account access where a development account can assume a role in the production account to deploy applications. The access must require MFA, be time-limited to 1 hour, and be logged for audit. Only specific IAM users in the dev account should be allowed to assume the role.
toolboxServices: AWS IAM|AWS STS|AWS CloudTrail|AWS Organizations|Amazon Cognito|AWS SSO (IAM Identity Center)|AWS RAM
correctPipelineOrder: AWS IAM|AWS STS|AWS CloudTrail
hint: IAM role trust policies in the production account define which principals in the dev account can assume the role, with MFA conditions.
hint2: STS AssumeRole returns temporary credentials with a configurable DurationSeconds (max 1 hour) that automatically expire.
explanation: AWS IAM creates the cross-account role in the production account with a trust policy specifying the dev account as the trusted principal, including the condition aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent:true to enforce MFA and restricting to specific IAM user ARNs. AWS STS AssumeRole is called by authorized dev account users with MFA token, returning temporary credentials (access key, secret key, session token) with DurationSeconds set to 3600 (1 hour) that automatically expire. AWS CloudTrail logs both the AssumeRole API call in the dev account and all subsequent API calls made with the temporary credentials in the production account, providing the complete audit trail for compliance.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to protect their public-facing web application from OWASP Top 10 attacks (SQL injection, XSS), rate-limit abusive clients, block requests from known malicious IP ranges, and implement bot detection — while maintaining low latency for legitimate users globally.
toolboxServices: AWS WAF|Amazon CloudFront|AWS Shield Advanced|AWS Firewall Manager|Amazon API Gateway|AWS Network Firewall|Elastic Load Balancing (ALB)
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon CloudFront|AWS WAF|AWS Shield Advanced|Elastic Load Balancing (ALB)
hint: CloudFront is the first layer — it serves as the global edge network where WAF rules are evaluated before traffic reaches your origin.
hint2: WAF managed rule groups (AWSManagedRulesCommonRuleSet, AWSManagedRulesSQLiRuleSet, AWSManagedRulesBotControlRuleSet) provide pre-built OWASP protection.
explanation: Amazon CloudFront serves as the global edge network, providing low-latency content delivery and acting as the first inspection point where AWS WAF rules are evaluated at the edge — before traffic ever reaches the origin. AWS WAF attached to CloudFront applies managed rule groups for SQL injection (AWSManagedRulesSQLiRuleSet), XSS (AWSManagedRulesCommonRuleSet), bot detection (AWSManagedRulesBotControlRuleSet), rate-based rules for abusive clients, and IP set rules blocking known malicious ranges. AWS Shield Advanced provides DDoS protection with 24/7 DDoS Response Team (DRT) support, cost protection during attacks, and advanced attack visibility. Elastic Load Balancing (ALB) serves as the origin, distributing traffic to the application tier with security group restrictions allowing only CloudFront IP ranges.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to implement automated incident response when GuardDuty detects an EC2 instance communicating with a known command-and-control server. The instance must be automatically isolated (no inbound/outbound traffic except forensic access), a forensic snapshot must be captured, and the security team must be notified — all within 3 minutes.
toolboxServices: Amazon GuardDuty|Amazon EventBridge|AWS Lambda|AWS Step Functions|Amazon EC2|Amazon SNS|AWS Systems Manager
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon GuardDuty|Amazon EventBridge|AWS Step Functions|AWS Lambda|Amazon SNS
hint: GuardDuty generates a Backdoor:EC2/C&CActivity.B finding that publishes to EventBridge for automated response.
hint2: Step Functions orchestrates the multi-step response — isolation, snapshot, tagging, notification — with error handling and parallel execution.
explanation: Amazon GuardDuty detects the C&C communication and generates a Backdoor:EC2/C&CActivity.B finding that is automatically published to Amazon EventBridge. EventBridge matches the finding type and severity with a rule that triggers AWS Step Functions to orchestrate the incident response workflow. Step Functions executes parallel branches — AWS Lambda functions replace the instance's security groups with a forensic isolation SG (deny all except forensic team CIDR), create EBS snapshots for forensic analysis, tag the instance as compromised, and disable any associated IAM instance profile. Amazon SNS sends a formatted notification to the security team with finding details, instance metadata, and forensic snapshot IDs within the 3-minute SLA.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to implement end-to-end encryption for data flowing from IoT sensors through an API, processed by Lambda, and stored in DynamoDB. Encryption keys must be rotated annually, different keys must be used for different data classifications (public, confidential, restricted), and all key usage must be auditable.
toolboxServices: AWS KMS|AWS CloudHSM|Amazon DynamoDB|AWS Lambda|Amazon API Gateway|AWS Certificate Manager|AWS Secrets Manager
correctPipelineOrder: AWS Certificate Manager|Amazon API Gateway|AWS Lambda|AWS KMS|Amazon DynamoDB
hint: ACM provides the TLS certificate for API Gateway, encrypting data in transit from IoT sensors to the API endpoint.
hint2: KMS customer-managed keys with separate key policies per data classification (public/confidential/restricted) enforce key segregation and annual rotation.
explanation: AWS Certificate Manager provisions and manages the TLS certificate attached to Amazon API Gateway, encrypting data in transit from IoT sensors to the API endpoint. Amazon API Gateway receives the encrypted HTTPS requests and invokes AWS Lambda for processing. AWS Lambda processes the data and uses AWS KMS to encrypt fields with classification-specific customer-managed keys — separate CMKs for public, confidential, and restricted data, each with annual automatic rotation enabled and key policies restricting access to authorized roles. Amazon DynamoDB stores the encrypted data with KMS encryption at rest, and CloudTrail logs every KMS Encrypt/Decrypt/GenerateDataKey API call for complete key usage auditability.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to implement a VPC architecture where EC2 instances in private subnets can access S3 and DynamoDB without traversing the internet, access specific third-party SaaS APIs through a controlled egress path, and all network traffic must be logged and inspectable for data exfiltration detection.
toolboxServices: VPC Gateway Endpoint|VPC Interface Endpoint|NAT Gateway|AWS Network Firewall|VPC Flow Logs|AWS PrivateLink|Internet Gateway
correctPipelineOrder: VPC Gateway Endpoint|VPC Interface Endpoint|AWS Network Firewall|VPC Flow Logs
hint: Gateway Endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB route traffic through the AWS private network via route table entries — no internet required.
hint2: Network Firewall inspects egress traffic to third-party SaaS APIs with domain filtering rules, detecting and blocking data exfiltration attempts.
explanation: VPC Gateway Endpoints provide private connectivity to S3 and DynamoDB through route table entries, keeping traffic entirely within the AWS network without NAT Gateway or internet traversal — with endpoint policies restricting access to specific buckets and tables. VPC Interface Endpoints (powered by AWS PrivateLink) provide private access to other AWS services (CloudWatch, KMS, STS) via ENIs in the private subnet. AWS Network Firewall inspects all egress traffic destined for third-party SaaS APIs with stateful domain filtering rules (allow-listing specific domains), TLS inspection for encrypted traffic, and IPS signatures detecting data exfiltration patterns. VPC Flow Logs capture all network traffic metadata (source, destination, ports, bytes, action) for security analysis and anomaly detection.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to implement a secrets management strategy where database credentials are automatically rotated every 30 days, applications retrieve secrets at runtime without hardcoding, rotation must not cause application downtime, and all secret access must be auditable with alerts on unauthorized access attempts.
toolboxServices: AWS Secrets Manager|AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store|AWS KMS|AWS Lambda|AWS CloudTrail|Amazon EventBridge|AWS IAM
correctPipelineOrder: AWS Secrets Manager|AWS Lambda|AWS KMS|AWS CloudTrail|Amazon EventBridge
hint: Secrets Manager natively supports automatic rotation with configurable schedules and built-in rotation Lambda functions for RDS, Redshift, and DocumentDB.
hint2: The rotation Lambda function uses a multi-user rotation strategy (alternating users) to ensure zero-downtime during credential rotation.
explanation: AWS Secrets Manager stores database credentials with automatic rotation configured for 30-day intervals, and applications retrieve secrets at runtime using the GetSecretValue API with IAM role-based access — no hardcoded credentials. AWS Lambda rotation functions implement the multi-user rotation strategy (creating alternating user credentials) ensuring zero application downtime during rotation, as the previous credential remains valid until the new one is fully tested. AWS KMS customer-managed keys encrypt all secrets at rest with key policies restricting decrypt permissions to authorized application roles. AWS CloudTrail logs every GetSecretValue, RotateSecret, and PutSecretValue API call, and Amazon EventBridge captures rotation failure events and unauthorized access attempts (AccessDenied on GetSecretValue) to trigger security alerts.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to implement a container security strategy for their EKS cluster. Container images must be scanned for vulnerabilities before deployment, pods must run with least-privilege IAM permissions, network traffic between pods must be encrypted and segmented, and runtime threats must be detected.
toolboxServices: Amazon ECR|Amazon Inspector|Amazon GuardDuty EKS Protection|AWS IAM Roles for Service Accounts (IRSA)|Kubernetes Network Policies|Amazon EKS|AWS App Mesh
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon ECR|Amazon Inspector|AWS IAM Roles for Service Accounts (IRSA)|Kubernetes Network Policies|Amazon GuardDuty EKS Protection
hint: ECR stores container images and integrates with Inspector for automated vulnerability scanning on push — before images reach the cluster.
hint2: IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts) maps Kubernetes service accounts to IAM roles, providing pod-level least-privilege AWS permissions.
explanation: Amazon ECR stores container images with immutable tags and scan-on-push enabled, triggering Amazon Inspector to automatically scan images for OS and programming language vulnerabilities with severity scoring — blocking deployment of images with critical CVEs via admission controllers. AWS IAM Roles for Service Accounts (IRSA) maps each Kubernetes service account to a specific IAM role with least-privilege permissions, ensuring pods only access the AWS resources they need without sharing node-level credentials. Kubernetes Network Policies segment pod-to-pod traffic by namespace and label selectors, enforcing micro-segmentation that prevents lateral movement between services. Amazon GuardDuty EKS Protection monitors EKS audit logs and runtime activity to detect threats — privilege escalation, crypto mining, anonymous access, and suspicious API calls from compromised pods.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to implement a secure logging architecture where CloudTrail logs from 150 accounts are stored immutably in a centralized security account. Logs must be tamper-proof (no one can delete or modify them, including administrators), encrypted with a security-team-controlled key, and searchable within 5 minutes of generation.
toolboxServices: AWS CloudTrail|Amazon S3|S3 Object Lock|AWS KMS|Amazon CloudWatch Logs|Amazon OpenSearch Service|AWS Organizations
correctPipelineOrder: AWS CloudTrail|Amazon S3|S3 Object Lock|AWS KMS|Amazon OpenSearch Service
hint: CloudTrail organization trail automatically collects logs from all 150 accounts and delivers them to a centralized S3 bucket in the security account.
hint2: S3 Object Lock in compliance mode creates WORM (Write Once Read Many) protection that prevents deletion by anyone — including root — until retention expires.
explanation: AWS CloudTrail creates an organization trail that automatically captures all management and data events from 150 accounts and delivers them to a centralized Amazon S3 bucket in the security account with CloudTrail log file integrity validation enabled. S3 Object Lock in compliance mode enforces WORM protection — no one, including root users and AWS support, can delete or modify log files until the retention period expires, ensuring tamper-proof immutability. AWS KMS encrypts all logs with a customer-managed key controlled by the security team, with key policies denying decrypt access to non-security roles. Amazon OpenSearch Service ingests logs via S3 event notifications and Lambda, providing full-text search and visualization within 5 minutes of log generation.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to implement a secure CI/CD pipeline where infrastructure-as-code templates are scanned for security misconfigurations before deployment, container images are scanned for vulnerabilities, secrets are never hardcoded in code, and all deployments require security team approval for production.
toolboxServices: AWS CodePipeline|AWS CodeBuild|cfn-nag|Amazon ECR|Amazon Inspector|AWS Secrets Manager|AWS CodeDeploy
correctPipelineOrder: AWS CodePipeline|AWS CodeBuild|cfn-nag|Amazon Inspector|AWS Secrets Manager|AWS CodeDeploy
hint: CodePipeline orchestrates the security gates — each stage must pass before the pipeline advances to the next.
hint2: cfn-nag performs static analysis on CloudFormation templates, detecting insecure patterns like open security groups and unencrypted resources.
explanation: AWS CodePipeline orchestrates the secure CI/CD workflow with mandatory security gates at each stage, including a manual approval action requiring security team sign-off before production deployment. AWS CodeBuild executes the build stage, running cfn-nag for static security analysis of CloudFormation templates — detecting open security groups, unencrypted resources, overly permissive IAM policies, and missing logging configurations. Amazon Inspector scans container images pushed to ECR for OS and language-level vulnerabilities, failing the pipeline if critical CVEs are found. AWS Secrets Manager provides runtime secret injection during deployment (database credentials, API keys) so secrets are never hardcoded in source code or templates, and AWS CodeDeploy executes the production deployment with blue/green strategy and automatic rollback.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to implement a DDoS protection strategy for their multi-tier application. The architecture includes CloudFront distributions, Application Load Balancers, and EC2 instances. They need automatic DDoS detection, mitigation, cost protection during attacks, and access to the AWS DDoS Response Team for complex attacks.
toolboxServices: AWS Shield Standard|AWS Shield Advanced|AWS WAF|Amazon CloudFront|Amazon Route 53|Elastic Load Balancing (ALB)|AWS Firewall Manager
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon Route 53|Amazon CloudFront|AWS WAF|AWS Shield Advanced|Elastic Load Balancing (ALB)
hint: Route 53 is the first layer of defense — it provides DNS-level DDoS protection and health-check-based failover.
hint2: Shield Advanced must be associated with specific resources (CloudFront, ALB, Route 53, EIP) to provide enhanced DDoS protection and cost protection.
explanation: Amazon Route 53 provides the DNS layer with built-in DDoS protection (shuffle sharding, anycast routing) and health-check-based failover to redirect traffic during attacks. Amazon CloudFront absorbs volumetric DDoS attacks at the edge with its massive global network capacity, keeping attack traffic away from the origin. AWS WAF on CloudFront provides application-layer (Layer 7) protection with rate-based rules that automatically block abusive IPs and managed rule groups filtering malicious request patterns. AWS Shield Advanced provides enhanced DDoS detection, automatic mitigation for network/transport layer attacks, cost protection (credits for scaling charges during attacks), and 24/7 access to the DDoS Response Team (DRT). Elastic Load Balancing (ALB) distributes surviving legitimate traffic to the application tier with security groups restricting access to CloudFront IP ranges only.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to implement identity federation so that 5,000 corporate employees can access AWS accounts using their existing Active Directory credentials. Users should be assigned different permission levels based on their AD group membership, and access must be centrally managed across 80 AWS accounts.
toolboxServices: AWS IAM Identity Center (SSO)|AWS Organizations|Active Directory Connector|Amazon Cognito|AWS IAM|AWS Directory Service|SAML 2.0
correctPipelineOrder: AWS Directory Service|AWS IAM Identity Center (SSO)|AWS Organizations
hint: AWS Directory Service (AD Connector) creates a proxy to your on-premises Active Directory without replicating directory data to AWS.
hint2: IAM Identity Center maps AD groups to permission sets, automatically provisioning access across all 80 accounts based on group membership.
explanation: AWS Directory Service deploys an AD Connector that creates a secure proxy connection to the on-premises Active Directory, enabling AWS services to authenticate users against existing corporate credentials without replicating directory data. AWS IAM Identity Center (SSO) integrates with the AD Connector as its identity source, mapping Active Directory groups to permission sets — each group (Developers, DBAs, Security) receives different IAM permission sets that define their access level. AWS Organizations provides the multi-account structure where IAM Identity Center automatically provisions the mapped permission sets across all 80 accounts, so when a user is added to an AD group, they immediately receive the corresponding access across all relevant accounts.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to detect and prevent data exfiltration from their AWS environment. They need to monitor for unusual S3 data transfer patterns, detect unauthorized API calls to copy snapshots or share AMIs to external accounts, and block DNS-based data exfiltration tunneling.
toolboxServices: Amazon GuardDuty|Amazon Macie|AWS CloudTrail|Amazon Route 53 Resolver DNS Firewall|VPC Flow Logs|Amazon Detective|AWS Network Firewall
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon GuardDuty|Amazon Macie|Amazon Route 53 Resolver DNS Firewall|VPC Flow Logs|Amazon Detective
hint: GuardDuty detects data exfiltration patterns including unusual S3 access, snapshot copying to external accounts, and DNS-based tunneling.
hint2: Route 53 Resolver DNS Firewall blocks DNS queries to known malicious domains and detects DNS tunneling patterns used for data exfiltration.
explanation: Amazon GuardDuty analyzes CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS logs to detect data exfiltration patterns — Exfiltration:S3/MaliciousIPCaller, Stealth:S3/ServerAccessLoggingDisabled, and unauthorized snapshot sharing (Recon:EC2/PortProbeUnprotectedPort). Amazon Macie continuously monitors S3 buckets for sensitive data exposure, detecting when classified data is moved to unencrypted or publicly accessible locations. Amazon Route 53 Resolver DNS Firewall blocks DNS queries to known malicious domains and detects DNS tunneling patterns (unusually long subdomain queries encoding exfiltrated data). VPC Flow Logs capture all network traffic metadata for analysis of unusual data transfer volumes and connections to suspicious external IPs. Amazon Detective correlates all these signals into an investigation graph, enabling security analysts to trace the full exfiltration path from initial access to data theft.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to implement a secure multi-account architecture where SCPs prevent any account from disabling CloudTrail, GuardDuty, or Config. Developers must be prevented from creating IAM users (only roles allowed), and no resources can be launched outside of approved regions (us-east-1, eu-west-1).
toolboxServices: AWS Organizations|Service Control Policies (SCPs)|AWS IAM|AWS Config|AWS Control Tower|AWS CloudFormation|AWS Service Catalog
correctPipelineOrder: AWS Organizations|Service Control Policies (SCPs)|AWS Config|AWS Control Tower
hint: SCPs are deny-based guardrails attached to OUs that restrict what actions member accounts can perform — even if IAM policies allow them.
hint2: An SCP with Deny on cloudtrail:StopLogging, guardduty:DisableDetector, and config:StopConfigurationRecorder prevents security service tampering.
explanation: AWS Organizations provides the OU hierarchy where SCPs are attached at different levels — security services OU, workload OUs, and sandbox OUs each receive appropriate restrictions. Service Control Policies (SCPs) implement three critical deny policies: (1) deny StopLogging/DeleteTrail/DisableDetector/StopConfigurationRecorder to prevent security service tampering, (2) deny CreateUser/CreateAccessKey to force role-based access only, and (3) deny all actions with a condition on aws:RequestedRegion not in [us-east-1, eu-west-1] to enforce region restrictions. AWS Config provides detective controls with conformance packs that continuously verify the preventive SCPs are working — detecting any non-compliant resources that somehow bypass the guardrails. AWS Control Tower provides the overall governance framework with mandatory guardrails that implement these SCPs as part of the landing zone baseline.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to implement TLS termination for their internal microservices running on ECS Fargate. Each service needs its own certificate, certificates must be automatically rotated before expiration, and all inter-service communication must use mutual TLS (mTLS) for zero-trust authentication.
toolboxServices: AWS Certificate Manager (ACM)|AWS Private Certificate Authority (PCA)|Amazon ECS Fargate|AWS App Mesh|Elastic Load Balancing (ALB)|Amazon API Gateway|AWS Secrets Manager
correctPipelineOrder: AWS Private Certificate Authority (PCA)|AWS Certificate Manager (ACM)|AWS App Mesh|Amazon ECS Fargate
hint: Private CA issues private certificates for internal services — unlike ACM public certificates, these can be used for mTLS client authentication.
hint2: App Mesh Envoy sidecar proxies handle mTLS negotiation transparently, so application code doesn't need to implement TLS logic.
explanation: AWS Private Certificate Authority (PCA) creates a private CA hierarchy (root + subordinate) that issues private X.509 certificates for each microservice — private CAs are required for mTLS because both client and server certificates must be issued by a trusted internal authority. AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) manages the lifecycle of certificates issued by PCA, providing automatic renewal before expiration without manual intervention. AWS App Mesh deploys Envoy sidecar proxies alongside each Fargate task, configured with the PCA-issued certificates to enforce mutual TLS — every service-to-service call requires both client and server certificate validation, implementing zero-trust authentication. Amazon ECS Fargate runs the microservices with the App Mesh sidecar handling all TLS negotiation transparently.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to implement a secure data classification and protection strategy for their S3 data lake containing 50 TB of mixed data. They need to automatically discover and classify sensitive data (PII, PHI, financial), apply appropriate encryption based on classification, and continuously monitor for policy violations like public access or cross-account sharing.
toolboxServices: Amazon Macie|Amazon S3|AWS KMS|AWS Lambda|AWS Config|Amazon EventBridge|AWS Security Hub
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon Macie|Amazon EventBridge|AWS Lambda|AWS KMS|AWS Security Hub
hint: Macie uses ML and pattern matching to automatically discover and classify sensitive data types (PII, PHI, financial) across all S3 buckets.
hint2: EventBridge captures Macie findings and routes them to Lambda for automated remediation — applying encryption, fixing permissions, or quarantining objects.
explanation: Amazon Macie scans the 50 TB data lake using ML-based classifiers and managed data identifiers to automatically discover and classify sensitive data — PII (SSN, email, phone), PHI (medical record numbers, health plan IDs), and financial data (credit card numbers, bank accounts) — generating findings with severity ratings. Amazon EventBridge captures Macie findings and routes them based on classification and severity to AWS Lambda for automated remediation actions. AWS Lambda applies classification-appropriate encryption using AWS KMS — public data uses AWS-managed keys, confidential data uses department-specific CMKs, and restricted data uses CMKs with strict key policies limiting access to authorized roles only. AWS Security Hub aggregates all Macie findings alongside Config compliance data for centralized visibility into the data protection posture.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to implement a secure network architecture for a PCI DSS compliant payment processing application. The cardholder data environment (CDE) must be completely isolated, all traffic in and out must be inspected, access must be restricted to specific IP ranges, and all network flows must be logged with 1-year retention.
toolboxServices: Amazon VPC|AWS Network Firewall|VPC Flow Logs|Network ACLs|Security Groups|AWS WAF|AWS Transit Gateway
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon VPC|Network ACLs|Security Groups|AWS Network Firewall|VPC Flow Logs
hint: A dedicated VPC for the CDE provides network-level isolation — no shared infrastructure with non-PCI workloads.
hint2: Network ACLs provide stateless subnet-level filtering as the first defense layer, while Security Groups provide stateful instance-level filtering.
explanation: Amazon VPC creates a completely isolated network for the cardholder data environment (CDE) with private subnets only — no internet gateway, no public subnets — meeting PCI DSS Requirement 1 for network segmentation. Network ACLs provide stateless subnet-level filtering as the outer defense perimeter, restricting traffic to specific IP ranges (payment processors, admin jump boxes) and denying all other inbound/outbound traffic. Security Groups provide stateful instance-level micro-segmentation, allowing only required ports (443 for payment API, 5432 for database) between specific security group references. AWS Network Firewall performs deep packet inspection on all traffic entering and leaving the CDE VPC, with IPS signatures detecting payment card data patterns in network flows. VPC Flow Logs capture all network traffic metadata with 1-year retention in S3 for PCI DSS audit requirements.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to implement a privileged access management strategy where administrators can only access production EC2 instances through a controlled, auditable session — no SSH keys, no bastion hosts, no open inbound ports. All commands must be logged, sessions must be time-limited, and access must require approval.
toolboxServices: AWS Systems Manager Session Manager|AWS IAM|AWS CloudTrail|Amazon S3|Amazon CloudWatch Logs|AWS Systems Manager Change Manager|EC2 Instance Connect
correctPipelineOrder: AWS Systems Manager Change Manager|AWS IAM|AWS Systems Manager Session Manager|Amazon S3|Amazon CloudWatch Logs
hint: Session Manager provides browser-based shell access to EC2 instances through the SSM Agent — no SSH keys, no bastion hosts, no inbound ports required.
hint2: Change Manager enforces an approval workflow before administrators can initiate Session Manager sessions to production instances.
explanation: AWS Systems Manager Change Manager enforces a formal approval workflow — administrators must submit a change request specifying the target instance and justification, which requires security team approval before any production access is granted. AWS IAM policies restrict Session Manager access with conditions (ssm:resourceTag/Environment:production requires the approved change request ID), ensuring only approved sessions can be initiated. AWS Systems Manager Session Manager provides secure, auditable shell access through the SSM Agent — no SSH keys to manage, no bastion hosts to maintain, no inbound security group rules to open (port 22 remains closed). Amazon S3 stores complete session logs (every keystroke, command, and output) with Object Lock for tamper-proof retention, and Amazon CloudWatch Logs provides real-time session monitoring with metric filters alerting on suspicious commands (rm -rf, chmod 777, curl to external IPs).
